title: CC Exercises — Platform Features
description: Hands-on exercises covering ImpEx scripting, CronJob creation, cache configuration, Solr indexing, and the event system.

sections.title: Exercise 1 — ImpEx Data Loading & Scripting
sections.content:
Objective

Write ImpEx scripts that create, update, and remove data — covering INSERT_UPDATE, REMOVE, header modifiers, macros, and scripting hooks.

Steps

  1. Basic INSERT_UPDATE: Write an ImpEx that creates 5 LoyaltyCard instances with different card numbers, points, and tiers. Use the INSERT_UPDATE mode so running it twice is idempotent.
  2. Macros: Define a macro $catalogVersion at the top of your script. Use it to create 3 Products in the Staged version of a product catalog. Demonstrate how macros reduce repetition.
  3. Translators & special syntax: Write an ImpEx that creates a Product with a localised name in English and French (use the [lang=en] and [lang=fr] modifiers). Add an image using a media reference.
  4. REMOVE: Write an ImpEx that removes the 5 LoyaltyCards you created in step 1. Verify they no longer appear in FlexibleSearch.
  5. Scripting hook: Write an ImpEx with a #% beforeEach block that logs a message for every row processed. Run it via HAC and verify the log output.

Validation

Run each ImpEx via HAC → Console → ImpEx Import. Verify results with FlexibleSearch queries. Run the INSERT_UPDATE script twice and confirm no duplicates are created (idempotency). Export some of the data using ImpEx Export and verify the round-trip.

sections.title: Exercise 2 — Create a Custom CronJob
sections.content:
Objective

Build a custom CronJob that performs a scheduled business task — querying data, processing it, and logging results.

Scenario

Create a CronJob that finds all LoyaltyCards where points have changed in the last 24 hours and recalculates the tier based on the current points value.

Steps

  1. In items.xml, create a LoyaltyTierRecalcCronJob type extending CronJob. No additional attributes needed (it uses the base CronJob fields).
  2. Create a LoyaltyTierRecalcJobPerformable class extending AbstractJobPerformable<LoyaltyTierRecalcCronJobModel>.
  3. In the perform() method:
    • Query all LoyaltyCards using FlexibleSearch (or your LoyaltyCardDao).
    • For each card, calculate the correct tier based on points (BRONZE/SILVER/GOLD/PLATINUM thresholds from Exercise 2 of Tier 1).
    • If the tier has changed, update it via ModelService and log the change.
    • Return CronJobResult.SUCCESS and CronJobStatus.FINISHED.
  4. Register the performable as a Spring bean.
  5. Create the CronJob instance and a Trigger via ImpEx (set to run every hour or use a cron expression).

Validation

Manually create LoyaltyCards with mismatched points/tier (e.g. 6000 points but BRONZE tier). Run the CronJob from Backoffice or HAC. Verify the tiers are recalculated. Check the CronJob log for the entries you logged. Verify the trigger schedule is set correctly.

sections.title: Exercise 3 — Solr Search Configuration
sections.content:
Objective

Configure Solr indexing for a product attribute, run the indexer, and verify the attribute appears in search results and faceted navigation.

Steps

  1. Choose a product attribute to index — either a standard one (like manufacturer) or a custom one you've added to the Product type. For this exercise, let's use a custom attribute warrantyMonths (Integer) that you add to Product in items.xml.
  2. Navigate to Backoffice → System → Solr Facet Search (or configure via ImpEx). Find the Solr indexed type configuration for your storefront's products.
  3. Add a new indexed property:
    • Name: warrantyMonths
    • Type: int
    • Field value provider: modelAttributeFieldValueProvider (the standard provider for simple item attributes)
    • Mark it as facet (to enable faceted filtering on the storefront)
  4. Run a full Solr index for the product type (from Backoffice or via the Solr indexer CronJob).
  5. Verify in the Solr admin console (if accessible) or via the storefront search that the warranty field is indexed and the facet appears.

Bonus Challenge

Create a custom FieldValueProvider that indexes a calculated value — for example, a "priceRange" field that returns "budget", "mid-range", or "premium" based on the product's base price. Register it as a Spring bean and use it as the field value provider for a new indexed property.

Validation

After indexing, search for products on the storefront. Verify the warranty facet appears in the filter sidebar. Click a facet value and verify only products with matching warranty months are returned. If you built the custom provider, verify the price range facet works correctly.

sections.title: Exercise 4 — Publish & Listen to a Custom Event
sections.content:
Objective

Create a custom event, publish it from a service, and implement a listener that reacts to it — demonstrating the decoupled event-driven architecture.

Scenario

When a LoyaltyCard's tier changes (e.g. from SILVER to GOLD), publish a LoyaltyTierChangedEvent. A listener reacts by logging the change and (simulating) sending a congratulations email.

Steps

  1. Create a LoyaltyTierChangedEvent class extending AbstractEvent. Add fields: cardNumber (String), previousTier (LoyaltyTier), newTier (LoyaltyTier), customerUid (String).
  2. In your LoyaltyService.addPoints() method (from Tier 1 exercises), after recalculating the tier, check if it changed. If it did, publish the event:
    eventService.publishEvent(new LoyaltyTierChangedEvent(
    card.getCardNumber(), oldTier, newTier, customer.getUid()));
  3. Create a LoyaltyTierChangedEventListener extending AbstractEventListener<LoyaltyTierChangedEvent>. In onEvent(), log the tier change details. Simulate sending an email by logging "Would send email to {customerUid}: Congratulations on reaching {newTier}!"
  4. Register the listener in *-spring.xml with parent="abstractEventListener".

Bonus Challenge

Make the listener asynchronous by overriding isAsynchronous() to return true. Add a 2-second Thread.sleep in the listener to simulate a slow email API. Verify that the addPoints() call returns immediately without waiting for the listener.

Validation

In HAC Groovy console: add enough points to a LoyaltyCard to trigger a tier change. Check the server logs for both the tier change log from the service and the "would send email" log from the listener. Verify the event carries all the expected data (card number, old tier, new tier, customer UID).
